content:
Statisticians long studied **Var(θ̂)** of estimators—how sample-based estimates fluctuate across repeated sampling (**Cramér–Rao**, **Fisher** information). That framework treats randomness over **which data you observed**, not over a single outcome draw.

In supervised learning, the same idea applies to **learned predictors**: retrain on different samples and **ŷ** changes. **Hoerl & Kennard (1970)** popularized **ridge regression** explicitly to stabilize coefficients (reduce variance) at a small bias cost.

The modern **bias–variance decomposition** for prediction error entered ML consciousness through **Geman, Bienenstock & Doursat (1992)** on neural networks and was popularized for practitioners in **Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman (*Elements of Statistical Learning*)**.

**Breiman**'s **bagging (1996)** and **random forests (2001)** were motivated largely by **variance reduction** via averaging unstable trees.

Today, variance remains the vocabulary for **overfitting sensitivity**, **learning curves**, **cross-validation spread**, and **regularization**—linking classical estimation theory to everyday model tuning.

timeline.label: Estimator variance in statistics
timeline.description: Sampling theory quantifies Var(θ̂) across repeated datasets—precursor to Var(ŷ) in ML.

timeline.label: Ridge regression
timeline.description: Hoerl & Kennard trade a small bias increase for lower variance in linear coefficients.

timeline.label: Bias–variance for neural nets
timeline.description: Geman et al. decompose prediction error into bias, variance, and noise for learned models.

timeline.label: Ensembles for variance reduction
timeline.description: Bagging and random forests average high-variance base learners to stabilize predictions.
